content.text: What it is

Gradual rollout strategies use feature flags to progressively expose a change — a new model version, a revised prompt, a new tool integration, or a modified reasoning chain — to an increasing percentage of traffic or users, while continuously monitoring quality metrics. Instead of flipping a switch for all users simultaneously, the system advances the rollout through controlled phases, pausing or rolling back automatically if metrics breach defined thresholds.

In agentic AI systems, gradual rollout is especially important because the blast radius of a broken model version or a prompt regression extends across all in-flight agent sessions. A 1% canary that reveals a reasoning failure protects 99% of users while the team investigates. The feature flag controls the percentage split; the flag management platform's targeting rules determine which users fall in which bucket.
Why it matters

LLM-backed agents exhibit non-deterministic behavior that is difficult to fully exercise in a test environment. A model that performs well on a benchmark suite may degrade on the long tail of production user intents that benchmarks do not cover. Gradual rollout limits the user population exposed to an unknown regression while generating real-world signal that offline evaluation cannot replicate.

Rollout velocity is also a business concern. A team that can confidently ramp from 1% to 100% in 48 hours — because automated quality gates will halt a bad rollout — can ship model improvements faster than one that must manually validate each increment. The flag-based rollout mechanism is the operational foundation that makes iterative improvement safe.
Benefits
Blast radius limitation.
A regression at 5% canary affects 5% of users, not 100%. The team can roll back in seconds by toggling the flag.
Real-world signal.
Canary traffic exercises the full distribution of production inputs, surfacing issues that synthetic test suites miss.
Decoupled deployment from release.
Code can be merged and deployed in an off state and enabled independently of the deployment schedule, breaking the coupling between code freeze and feature availability.
A/B measurement.

Running two variants simultaneously at a known split produces comparable cohorts. Quality metrics (task completion rate, user satisfaction, token cost per task) can be measured against a control group.
Automated advance.
Rollout orchestrators can automatically increase the percentage when quality gates pass, eliminating the need for manual approval at each increment during routine rollouts.
Trade-offs and limitations
Consistency requirements.
Users must receive the same variant across multiple requests in a session. Sticky assignments (pinning a user to a variant based on their user ID hash) are essential but complicate cleanup.
Metric lag.

Some quality degradations surface slowly (e.g., a model that generates subtly incorrect summaries). Automated gates that trigger too quickly may advance past a bad state; gates that require too much data slow rollout velocity.
Infrastructure cost.
Running two model versions simultaneously doubles inference costs during the ramp period. For large-context models this cost is significant.
Test pollution in metrics.
Internal employees in the dogfood cohort have different usage patterns than external users. Metrics from internal cohorts are not representative of production quality.
Flag accumulation.
A rollout that pauses at 50% for weeks becomes a persistent split that complicates reasoning about the production system. Paused rollouts need escalation paths.
How it works

The flag evaluation engine assigns each request to a variant using a deterministic hash of the user ID (or session ID for unauthenticated traffic) modulo 100. A rollout percentage of 10 means that users whose hash falls in 0..9 receive the treatment; users in 10..99 receive the control. This assignment is sticky: the same user always receives the same variant as long as the percentage does not change.

Targeting rules can layer on top of the percentage split. A rule might say: first restrict to internal employees (by email domain), then to opted-in beta users, then open to 5% of all users, then ramp. Each phase has a gate: a set of metrics that must hold for a defined duration (e.g., error rate below 0.5% for 2 hours at 5000+ requests) before the rollout advances automatically.

The rollout orchestrator polls the quality gate metrics from the observability stack every few minutes. If the gate passes, it increments the rollout percentage by a configured step (e.g., 5%). If the gate fails, it halts the rollout and fires an alert. An on-call engineer decides whether to roll back, investigate, or override. Most flag platforms expose an API for this automation; alternatively, a CI/CD pipeline job can drive the percentage updates.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Gradual rollout architecture: All traffic flows to Flag Evaluation which hashes user ID to assign Control (old model, 90%) or Treatment (new model, 10%). Five rollout phases are shown: Internal dogfood (1%), Canary (5%), Early Access (25%), Ramp (50-90%), and GA (100%, flag removed). An Automated Gate evaluates error rate and latency thresholds before each advance. A rollback arrow shows any phase can instantly revert to 0%.

content.text: The flag evaluation layer sits in the hot path of every agent invocation. Sticky hashing ensures session consistency. Quality gates between phases are enforced by the rollout orchestrator, which reads from the observability stack and writes back to the flag platform API.
When to use it
Model version upgrades.
Switching from one LLM version to another without a validation period risks regressions that offline evals did not catch.
Prompt template changes.
A revised system prompt that improves average quality on benchmarks may degrade a subset of intents. Canary rollout reveals the long-tail impact.
New tool integrations.
Enabling a new external tool for an agent (e.g., a database write capability) has irreversible side effects. Canary limits exposure while trust is established.
Reasoning chain modifications.
Changes to the agent's planning loop or tool-calling strategy affect all downstream task outcomes and need measured rollout.
How to implement
Define the rollout phases and gates.
Document the phase sequence (e.g., 1% internal, 5% canary, 25% early access, 50%, 90%, 100%) and the metric thresholds and observation window for each gate.
Instrument the agent for variant tracking.
Log the assigned variant alongside every quality metric event so that metrics can be segmented by control vs. treatment.
Use sticky hashing for session consistency.
Hash on user ID, not on request ID, so a user does not experience both variants within a session.
Build or configure the rollout orchestrator.
LaunchDarkly's progressive delivery feature and Unleash's gradual rollout strategy handle this natively. For a custom setup, a CI/CD job that polls metrics and calls the flag API works.
Set rollback runbooks.
Document how to immediately set the flag to 0% and whom to notify. The rollback action must be executable by any on-call engineer in under two minutes.
Expire and remove the flag at GA.
Once the treatment is at 100% and metrics are stable, remove the flag and its evaluation call sites within one sprint. A permanent flag at 100% is dead weight.
Examples
Rollout orchestrator: automated phase advance

"""rollout_orchestrator.py

Advances a gradual rollout if quality gates pass.
Run as a cron job every 30 minutes.
"""
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable

import httpx  # flag platform API client


@dataclass
class RolloutPhase:
    percentage: int
    min_requests: int       # minimum sample size before gate is checked
    observation_hours: float  # how long metrics must hold
    max_error_rate: float   # e.g. 0.005 = 0.5%
    max_p95_latency_ms: float


PHASES = [
    RolloutPhase(1,   500,   1.0, 0.005, 3000),
    RolloutPhase(5,   2000,  2.0, 0.005, 3000),
    RolloutPhase(25,  5000,  4.0, 0.005, 3000),
    RolloutPhase(50,  10000, 4.0, 0.005, 3000),
    RolloutPhase(90,  20000, 8.0, 0.003, 2800),
    RolloutPhase(100, 0,     0,   1.0,   99999),  # final phase: always pass
]


def get_current_percentage(flag_key: str) -> int:
    resp = httpx.get(f"https://flags.internal/api/flags/{flag_key}")
    return resp.json()["rolloutPercentage"]


def get_metrics(flag_key: str, variant: str, hours: float) -> dict:
    """Return error_rate and p95_latency_ms for the treatment variant."""
    resp = httpx.get(
        "https://metrics.internal/query",
        params={"flag": flag_key, "variant": variant, "hours": hours}
    )
    return resp.json()


def advance_rollout(flag_key: str, new_pct: int) -> None:
    httpx.patch(
        f"https://flags.internal/api/flags/{flag_key}",
        json={"rolloutPercentage": new_pct},
    )
    print(f"Advanced {flag_key} to {new_pct}%")


def halt_and_alert(flag_key: str, phase: RolloutPhase, metrics: dict) -> None:
    print(f"GATE FAILED for {flag_key} at {phase.percentage}%: {metrics}")
    # Post to PagerDuty / Slack here


def run_rollout(flag_key: str) -> None:
    current_pct = get_current_percentage(flag_key)
    current_phase = next((p for p in PHASES if p.percentage >= current_pct), None)
    if current_phase is None or current_phase.percentage == 100:
        print("Rollout complete")
        return

    metrics = get_metrics(flag_key, "treatment", current_phase.observation_hours)
    if (metrics["request_count"] < current_phase.min_requests or
            metrics["error_rate"] > current_phase.max_error_rate or
            metrics["p95_latency_ms"] > current_phase.max_p95_latency_ms):
        halt_and_alert(flag_key, current_phase, metrics)
        return

    next_phases = [p for p in PHASES if p.percentage > current_pct]
    if next_phases:
        advance_rollout(flag_key, next_phases[0].percentage)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    run_rollout("agents.summarizer.use_claude_3_7")
Agent flag evaluation with variant logging

from openfeature import api
from openfeature.evaluation_context import EvaluationContext
import structlog

log = structlog.get_logger()
client = api.get_client("agent-service")


def get_model_for_request(user_id: str, session_id: str) -> str:
    """Evaluate the model-version flag and return the model identifier."""
    ctx = EvaluationContext(targeting_key=user_id, attributes={"session_id": session_id})
    details = client.get_string_details(
        flag_key="agents.summarizer.use_claude_3_7",
        default_value="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
        evaluation_context=ctx,
    )
    model = details.value
    log.info(
        "flag.evaluated",
        flag="agents.summarizer.use_claude_3_7",
        variant=details.variant,
        model=model,
        user_id=user_id,
    )
    return model
Real-world example

An enterprise AI team was upgrading their document analysis agent from one LLM version to the next. The new model improved average benchmark scores by 12% but had an unknown distribution on production documents, which varied significantly from the benchmark dataset (which overrepresented English-language technical documentation).

At 5% canary, the rollout orchestrator detected that error rates on French and German document inputs were 3x higher than the control. The orchestrator halted the rollout and filed an alert. Investigation revealed that the new model had a regression on non-English tokenizer edge cases in the document pre-processing prompt. The team fixed the prompt, redeployed, and restarted the canary. The second attempt advanced to 100% in 72 hours without issues — a process that would have affected all users simultaneously under the previous big-bang deployment model.
Common mistakes
Hashing on request ID instead of user ID.
This assigns different variants across requests in the same user session, creating inconsistent behavior.
Gates without minimum sample sizes.
A gate that checks error rate after 10 requests has high variance. Require at least 500-2000 requests at each phase before the gate is evaluated.
Not logging the variant with every metric event.
Without variant labels on metrics, you cannot segment control vs. treatment and cannot detect regressions.
Leaving the flag at 100% indefinitely.
Once the rollout is complete, the flag serves no purpose but adds evaluation overhead and increases the flag count. Remove it within one sprint of reaching GA.
Using the same phase schedule for all changes.
A cosmetic prompt wording change can advance faster than a new model version. Tailor the phase schedule to the risk level of the change.
Best practices
Always hash on user ID for sticky assignment. Never hash on request ID or timestamp.
Set minimum sample sizes at each gate before any metric thresholds are evaluated.
Log the assigned variant with every metric event to enable control-vs-treatment segmentation in observability tools.
Automate phase advance to remove human bottlenecks during routine rollouts, while keeping human approval for anomaly-triggered halts.
Define an explicit rollback runbook: who can roll back, what the command is, and what downstream systems need notification.
Remove the flag from code within one sprint of reaching 100% to prevent flag accumulation.
Segment quality metrics by user cohort (locale, plan tier, usage pattern) during canary to catch regressions that affect specific populations.
Performance and scalability

The flag evaluation step must be sub-millisecond. Use an in-process SDK that maintains a locally cached copy of flag state rather than calling the flag service per request. The local cache is updated via a streaming connection; a startup-time fetch ensures the cache is warm before the first request is served. Budget for two parallel inference endpoints during the ramp period: the control and treatment models both need capacity. Auto-scaling should account for the peak traffic that each variant can receive at maximum rollout percentage.

Flag platform
Built-in progressive delivery, approval workflows, and metric-based rollout gates

Flag platform
Gradual rollout strategy with flexible user bucketing; self-hostable
OpenFeature
SDK standard
Vendor-neutral API; swap backend without changing agent code
Grafana / Prometheus
Metrics
Query-based rollout gates using PromQL error-rate and latency expressions
Argo Rollouts
Kubernetes rollout
Canary and blue-green deployments at the pod level; complements flag-based traffic splitting

Security and reliability

Sticky user assignment must not leak variant information to users: a user should not be able to determine they are in a treatment cohort based on response differences. For sensitive safety-related features, prevent users from self-selecting into cohorts by disabling opt-in mechanisms. Ensure that flag evaluation uses the canonical user identity from the authenticated session, not a client-supplied value that could be spoofed to force variant assignment.

For reliability, define a fail-open or fail-closed behavior for flag evaluation failures. For autonomous agent features (code execution, data writes), fail-closed to the control variant. For cosmetic improvements, fail-open to the treatment is acceptable. Document the fail behavior in the flag's registry entry.
Key takeaways
Gradual rollout limits blast radius: a regression at 5% canary affects 5% of users, and a flag toggle reverts it in seconds.
Sticky hashing on user ID is essential for session consistency; never hash on request ID.
Quality gates between phases must specify both a minimum sample size and a metric threshold to avoid false confidence from small-sample measurements.
Log the assigned variant with every metric event; without variant labels you cannot detect treatment regressions.
Automate phase advance to maintain rollout velocity; reserve human approval for gate failures and anomaly-triggered halts.
Remove the flag from code within one sprint of reaching 100%; a flag at 100% indefinitely is dead weight that increases cognitive overhead.
Segment canary metrics by cohort characteristics (locale, plan tier) to catch regressions that affect specific user populations.
